Distinguish challenging, evaluating, clarifying, proposing, qualifying, seeking feedback. The post: Steve says: "What you've listed here are actually frontend tools, in PEP 517 land. They aren't implemented like that, of course, but in the modern world all of these tools would look at a directory of code, infer what tool is needed to compile/layout the files, and then take the compiled files and do their own arrangements. If they used a wheel as an intermediate step and then unpacked it to e.g. turn the files into a .app or .pex, they start to look a lot more like pip (which unpacks a wheel to integrate it into a Python environment) than setuptools (which takes sources and produces a wheel-shaped Python library)."

"[quote=\"Matthew Pleskow, post:4, topic:107565, username:MattP\"]\nSince I only provided examples for hatch targets in the original post, I went to go look for projects using distutils/[`setuptools.Command`](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/extension.html#setuptools.Command) to create these custom targets and found these.\n[/quote]\n\nWhat you've listed here are actually _frontend_ tools, in PEP 517 land. They aren't implemented like that, of course, but in the modern world all of these tools would look at a directory of code, infer what tool is needed to compile/layout the files, and then take the compiled files and do their own arrangements.
